diseases.name: Rice Blast (leaf and neck)
diseases.symptoms: Diamond or spindle-shaped lesions appear on leaves, with grey centres and brown borders. As the crop ages, the node just below the panicle turns black and rotten, and the whole panicle bends or breaks; grains in affected panicles stay empty or chaffy. Severe attacks look like a burnt patch in the field.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Cool nights with heavy dew, daytime temperatures of 25-28 C, high humidity above 90%, excess nitrogen, and dense canopies favour rapid spread.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Tricyclazole 75% WP at 0.6 g per litre of water as a preventive spray at panicle initiation, repeated 15 days later. Alternative: Isoprothiolane 40% EC at 1.5 ml per litre.
diseases.treatment_organic: Spray Pseudomonas fluorescens at 10 g per litre or fresh cow urine diluted 1:10 every 10 days. Avoid late-evening urea top-dress; balance N with potash.
diseases.dosage: Tricyclazole: 200 g per acre in 200 litres of water using a knapsack sprayer, directed at the upper canopy. Two sprays, 15 days apart.
diseases.safety_precautions: Wear gloves, mask, and full sleeves while spraying. Do not spray against wind. Pre-harvest interval 30 days. Keep livestock and children out of the field for 24 hours.

diseases.name: Rice Sheath Blight
diseases.symptoms: Greenish-grey, water-soaked oval lesions appear on the leaf sheath at or just above the water line. They enlarge into bleached patches with brown borders and creep upward to the flag leaf. Small white-to-brown sclerotia (mustard-seed-like balls) sit on lesions and float on the water.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Dense planting, high nitrogen, standing water above 5 cm, and humid weather above 28 C with cloudy days.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Validamycin 3% L at 2 ml per litre, or Hexaconazole 5% EC at 2 ml per litre, sprayed at the base of the canopy at first lesion sighting.
diseases.treatment_organic: Trichoderma viride at 5 g per litre as a foliar drench. Drain the field for 2-3 days to break humidity. Reduce nitrogen and avoid panicle-stage urea.
diseases.dosage: Validamycin: 400 ml per acre in 200 litres of water; direct nozzle to the lower canopy and base. Repeat after 12-15 days if lesions move past mid-tiller.
diseases.safety_precautions: Wear waterproof gloves and rubber boots. Do not enter sprayed field for 12 hours. Pre-harvest interval 21 days. Wash sprayer and clothes separately.

diseases.name: Rice Bacterial Leaf Blight
diseases.symptoms: Yellow to straw-coloured water-soaked streaks start from the leaf tip or wounded margin and run down the leaf. Whole leaves dry to a pale straw colour while the rest of the plant looks green. In nursery and young plants the entire seedling can wilt suddenly (kresek phase).
diseases.favourable_conditions: Heavy rain with strong wind that wounds leaves, water-logged fields, temperatures of 25-30 C, and high doses of nitrogen.
diseases.treatment_chemical: There is no fully reliable chemical cure. Spray Streptocycline 90% + Tetracycline 10% (Plantomycin) at 0.15 g per litre combined with Copper oxychloride 50% WP at 2.5 g per litre at first symptoms, repeat after 10 days.
diseases.treatment_organic: Drain excess water. Spray fresh cow-dung extract (1 kg in 10 litres, filtered) or Pseudomonas fluorescens at 10 g per litre. Resistant varieties (IR 64, ADT 38) are the surest control.
diseases.dosage: Plantomycin 30 g + Copper oxychloride 500 g per acre in 200 litres of water. Two sprays at 10-day interval.
diseases.safety_precautions: Antibiotic sprays must not drift to drinking water sources. Wear gloves and mask. Pre-harvest interval 21 days. Do not reuse antibiotic packs for any other purpose.

diseases.name: Brown Plant Hopper (Rice)
diseases.symptoms: Circular patches of plants turn yellow then brown and dry up — called hopper-burn. When the canopy is parted at the base, brown wedge-shaped insects and white nymphs are seen on the stem just above the water. Sooty mould grows on the lower leaves from honeydew.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Continuous flooding, dense planting, excess nitrogen, sheltered humid micro-climate at the plant base, and warm 25-30 C weather.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Imidacloprid 17.8% SL at 0.3 ml per litre, or Pymetrozine 50% WG at 0.6 g per litre, or Buprofezin 25% SC at 1.5 ml per litre directed to the base of the canopy.
diseases.treatment_organic: Drain the field for 3-4 days to expose hoppers. Release the predatory mirid bug (Cyrtorhinus lividipennis) where available. Spray neem oil 5 ml + soap 1 g per litre at base.
diseases.dosage: Imidacloprid: 60 ml per acre in 200 litres of water. Use a hollow-cone nozzle aimed low; one spray and reassess after 7 days.
diseases.safety_precautions: Imidacloprid is highly toxic to bees and fish — do not let runoff enter ponds. PPE required. Pre-harvest interval 30 days. Avoid pyrethroid sprays which trigger hopper resurgence.

diseases.name: Rice False Smut
diseases.symptoms: Individual grains in the panicle are replaced by velvety yellow-orange balls that turn olive-green to black as they mature. Only a few grains per panicle are usually affected, but the spore balls contaminate clean grain at threshing.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Light rain or heavy dew during flowering, cloudy weather, high nitrogen, and late-flowering varieties are most at risk.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Propiconazole 25% EC at 1 ml per litre sprayed at boot-leaf stage (5-7 days before heading). A second spray of Copper oxychloride 50% WP at 2.5 g per litre after 50% heading.
diseases.treatment_organic: Use clean, certified seed and treat with hot water (52 C for 10 minutes). Avoid late-evening nitrogen top-dress at booting. Burn or deep-bury infected panicles.
diseases.dosage: Propiconazole: 200 ml per acre in 200 litres of water. Single preventive spray timed to the flag-leaf-emerging stage.
diseases.safety_precautions: Do not handle smut balls bare-handed; spores can irritate skin and lungs. Wear mask and gloves. Pre-harvest interval 35 days for propiconazole.

diseases.name: Tomato Early Blight
diseases.symptoms: Brown spots with concentric rings (target-board pattern) appear first on the lowest leaves and spread upward. Affected leaves yellow around the spots and drop. Stem lesions are sunken and dark; fruit shows leathery sunken patches near the stem end.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Warm humid weather of 24-29 C, alternating wet-dry days, overhead irrigation, and stressed or older plants short on potash.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5 g per litre, or Chlorothalonil 75% WP at 2 g per litre, or Azoxystrobin 23% SC at 1 ml per litre. Rotate active ingredients each spray.
diseases.treatment_organic: Spray Trichoderma viride at 5 g per litre or 1% Bordeaux mixture every 10 days. Mulch with paddy straw to stop soil-splash. Remove and burn lower infected leaves.
diseases.dosage: Mancozeb: 1 kg per acre in 400 litres of water, full coverage including leaf undersides. Spray every 10-12 days, maximum 4 sprays in a season.
diseases.safety_precautions: Wear mask, gloves, and full sleeves. Do not harvest within 5 days of mancozeb spray. Do not graze livestock on sprayed plant residue.

diseases.name: Tomato Late Blight
diseases.symptoms: Large irregular grey-green water-soaked patches form on leaves, often starting at the leaf edge. In humid mornings a white downy mould appears on the underside of the patches. Stems blacken; green fruit develops greasy brown firm patches that can rot the whole fruit in 2-3 days.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Cool wet weather (15-22 C), nights with heavy dew, prolonged leaf wetness, and dense canopies. Common in the Nilgiris and during winter rains.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Metalaxyl 8% + Mancozeb 64% WP at 2.5 g per litre as soon as weather turns cool and damp. Alternative: Cymoxanil 8% + Mancozeb 64% WP at 3 g per litre.
diseases.treatment_organic: 1% Bordeaux mixture every 7 days during cool wet spells. Increase plant spacing for airflow. Stake plants and prune lower leaves to lift the canopy off the soil.
diseases.dosage: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb: 1 kg per acre in 400 litres of water; cover both leaf surfaces. Repeat every 7 days during a wet spell, max 3 sprays.
diseases.safety_precautions: PPE required. Pre-harvest interval 7 days. Do not spray during flowering hours; protect bees. Wash spray gear away from drinking-water sources.

diseases.name: Tomato Leaf Curl Virus (ToLCV)
diseases.symptoms: Young leaves curl upward and inward, become small, thick and leathery with yellow margins. Plants are severely stunted and bushy. Flowering is reduced and any fruit that sets is small and deformed. Whiteflies are usually seen on the underside of leaves.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Hot dry weather of 28-35 C with high whitefly populations, weedy field margins, and overlap between old and new tomato crops in the area.
diseases.treatment_chemical: There is no chemical cure for the virus. Control the whitefly vector with Diafenthiuron 50% WP at 1.2 g per litre or Spiromesifen 22.9% SC at 1 ml per litre. Rogue and burn infected plants in the first 30 days.
diseases.treatment_organic: Use yellow sticky traps at 20 per acre. Spray neem oil 5 ml + soap 1 g per litre weekly. Cover the nursery with 50-mesh insect-proof net. Plant resistant hybrids (Arka Rakshak, Arka Samrat).
diseases.dosage: Diafenthiuron: 240 g per acre in 200 litres of water, focused on leaf undersides. Two sprays 10 days apart at first whitefly sighting.
diseases.safety_precautions: Whitefly insecticides are toxic to bees and natural predators; spray only in early morning. PPE required. Pre-harvest interval 7 days. Destroy uprooted plants by burning, not composting.

diseases.name: Tomato Fruit Borer
diseases.symptoms: Round bore-holes on green and ripening fruit, often near the stem-end, with frass (brown excreta) at the hole. Inside, the fruit pulp is hollowed and rotting. Caterpillars are green to brown with light stripes and rest with their head buried inside the fruit.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Continuous tomato cropping, nearby chickpea or cotton fields, warm 25-30 C weather, and absence of pheromone-trap monitoring.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Chlorantraniliprole 18.5% SC at 0.3 ml per litre, or Emamectin benzoate 5% SG at 0.4 g per litre, or Spinosad 45% SC at 0.3 ml per litre. Rotate to avoid resistance.
diseases.treatment_organic: Install pheromone traps at 8-10 per acre. Spray HaNPV (Helicoverpa nucleopolyhedrovirus) at 250 LE per hectare in the evening. Hand-pick and destroy bored fruits; release Trichogramma chilonis at 50,000 per acre weekly.
diseases.dosage: Chlorantraniliprole: 60 ml per acre in 200 litres of water. Spray in the evening when larvae feed actively. Maximum 2 sprays per season.
diseases.safety_precautions: Pre-harvest interval 3 days for chlorantraniliprole, 5 days for emamectin. Wear PPE; some products are mild eye irritants. Do not spray on the morning of harvest.

diseases.name: Tomato Bacterial Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Plants suddenly wilt during the hottest part of the day while leaves stay green; recovery in the cool morning at first, then permanent wilt and collapse in 2-3 days. A cut stem placed in clear water releases a milky-white bacterial stream within 5 minutes — the classic field test.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Hot wet soil above 28 C, water-logging, root injury from hoeing or nematodes, acid sandy-loam soils, and short rotations with brinjal or chilli.
diseases.treatment_chemical: No reliable chemical cure once plants wilt. Drench beds before planting with bleaching powder at 15 kg per hectare or Streptocycline 0.2 g per litre + Copper oxychloride 2.5 g per litre. Roguing is essential.
diseases.treatment_organic: Rotate with paddy or maize for 2 seasons. Apply neem cake 250 kg per acre at land prep. Use grafted plants on resistant rootstock (brinjal cv. Mattu Gulla or wild Solanum torvum). Add lime to acidic soils.
diseases.dosage: Streptocycline + Copper oxychloride: drench 200 ml per plant at transplanting and again 30 days later, focused at the root zone.
diseases.safety_precautions: Sterilise tools between rows in 1% bleach. Do not wash hands or tools in the irrigation channel. Antibiotic drenches require gloves and mask.

diseases.name: Groundnut Tikka Leaf Spot (Early and Late)
diseases.symptoms: Round brown to black spots, 1-6 mm wide, appear on leaves with a yellow halo (early Tikka) or without halo on the underside (late Tikka). As spots merge, leaves yellow, dry, and drop, leaving bare stems. Heavy defoliation 2-3 weeks before harvest cuts pod yield sharply.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Warm humid weather of 25-30 C, frequent dew or light rain, and continuous groundnut cropping in the same field.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5 g per litre, or Chlorothalonil 75% WP at 2 g per litre, or Tebuconazole 25.9% EC at 1 ml per litre. Start at 30 days after sowing and repeat at 15-day intervals.
diseases.treatment_organic: Crop rotation with cereals for 2 seasons. Spray cow-urine 1:10 + neem oil 5 ml per litre. Use resistant varieties (ICGV 86590, TMV 13). Burn crop residue after harvest.
diseases.dosage: Mancozeb: 1 kg per acre in 200 litres of water. Two to three sprays at 30, 45 and 60 DAS depending on rainfall.
diseases.safety_precautions: PPE required. Pre-harvest interval 21 days. Do not spray during flowering hours to protect bees. Wash sprayer in a soak-pit, not in a canal.

diseases.name: Groundnut Rust
diseases.symptoms: Reddish-brown powdery pustules form on the underside of leaves; the upper surface shows pale yellow spots above each pustule. Leaves curl, dry, and drop, often together with late leaf spot. A finger rubbed on a leaf underside comes away rust-coloured.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Cool nights of 20-25 C, prolonged dew, dense canopies, late-sown crops, and overlap with infected nearby fields.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Hexaconazole 5% EC at 2 ml per litre, or Tebuconazole 25.9% EC at 1 ml per litre, or Propiconazole 25% EC at 1 ml per litre. Start at first pustule and spray twice 15 days apart.
diseases.treatment_organic: Spray sulphur 80% WP at 3 g per litre in cool weather (avoid above 32 C — leaf burn). Plough in volunteer groundnut. Use resistant ICGV-91114 or TMV 13.
diseases.dosage: Hexaconazole: 400 ml per acre in 200 litres of water. Two sprays at 15-day interval, both leaf surfaces covered.
diseases.safety_precautions: Wear mask and goggles; sulphur dust irritates eyes. Pre-harvest interval 25 days. Do not mix sulphur with oil-based products within 14 days — leaf burn.

diseases.name: Groundnut Bud Necrosis Virus
diseases.symptoms: Young leaves show necrotic ring spots and chlorotic streaks. The terminal bud blackens and dies, after which axillary buds shoot out together giving a 'bunchy top' look. Affected plants are stunted, do not flower, and produce no pods.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Heavy thrips populations, weedy field bunds, late-sown crops, hot dry weather of 28-35 C, and proximity to chilli or sunflower fields that host thrips.
diseases.treatment_chemical: No chemical cures the virus. Manage thrips vector with Fipronil 5% SC at 2 ml per litre or Spinosad 45% SC at 0.3 ml per litre at 15 and 30 DAS.
diseases.treatment_organic: Sow on time — early monsoon sowing escapes peak thrips. Border rows of pearl millet or sorghum act as a barrier crop. Rogue infected seedlings within 25 DAS. Mulch with paddy straw.
diseases.dosage: Fipronil: 400 ml per acre in 200 litres of water. Two foliar sprays 15 days apart in the first month.
diseases.safety_precautions: Fipronil is toxic to fish and bees — keep out of water bodies and avoid flowering-time sprays. PPE required. Pre-harvest interval 30 days.

diseases.name: Banana Panama Wilt (Fusarium Wilt)
diseases.symptoms: Older outer leaves yellow from the tip and margin, then collapse and hang around the pseudostem like a skirt. The pseudostem may split lengthwise near the base. A cross-cut of the corm or pseudostem shows reddish-brown to dark vascular streaks. Plants die before bunch maturity.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Re-planting bananas in infected soil, water-logged fields, acidic sandy soils, root injury by nematodes, and use of contaminated suckers from infected mats.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Soil drench with Carbendazim 50% WP at 2 g per litre at the base of the plant, 200 ml per plant, repeated every 2 months. Capsule injection of Carbendazim 50 mg in 3 ml water into the pseudostem at 4 and 6 months can suppress symptoms.
diseases.treatment_organic: Plant only tissue-culture or hot-water-treated suckers. Apply Trichoderma harzianum at 50 g + neem cake 1 kg per pit at planting. Maintain soil pH at 6.5-7.0 with lime. Rotate with paddy for 2-3 years where possible. Use resistant cv. Yangambi km5 or FHIA hybrids.
diseases.dosage: Carbendazim soil drench: 200 ml per plant of 0.2% solution every 60 days from planting until shooting.
diseases.safety_precautions: Carbendazim is a suspected reproductive toxicant — wear nitrile gloves and mask. Do not let runoff reach drinking water. Pre-harvest interval 60 days for fruit drench applications.

diseases.name: Banana Sigatoka Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Pale yellow streaks 2-3 mm long appear parallel to the leaf veins. They enlarge into elliptical brown spots with a grey centre and yellow halo. Lower leaves dry first, working upward; severe infection bleaches half the canopy and shortens fruit-fill. Premature ripening of fingers in the field is a warning sign.
diseases.favourable_conditions: High humidity above 90%, frequent rain, dense plantations, low-lying poorly drained soils, and overlap with infected ratoon crops.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Propiconazole 25% EC at 1 ml per litre + mineral oil 1% as a sticker, or Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5 g per litre. Spray the underside of leaves every 21 days during the wet season.
diseases.treatment_organic: Cut and burn yellowed lower leaves weekly. Spray Pseudomonas fluorescens at 10 g per litre. Maintain wide spacing (2 x 2 m) for airflow and ensure surface drainage.
diseases.dosage: Propiconazole + oil: 400 ml fungicide + 4 litres mineral oil per acre in 200 litres water. Maximum 4 sprays per crop, rotating with mancozeb.
diseases.safety_precautions: Mineral oil sprays can scorch leaves above 35 C — spray only in cool morning hours. PPE required. Pre-harvest interval 30 days for propiconazole.

diseases.name: Banana Bunchy Top Virus
diseases.symptoms: New leaves come out narrow, short, upright, and crowded at the top giving a rosette or 'bunchy' look. Dark green dot-and-dash streaks (Morse-code marks) run along the veins of leaves and the petiole. Plants stop growing and rarely produce a bunch; if they do, fingers are tiny and twisted.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Use of infected suckers from old plantations, presence of the banana aphid (Pentalonia nigronervosa), dense overlapping plantings, and lack of roguing.
diseases.treatment_chemical: No chemical cure. Manage the aphid vector with Imidacloprid 17.8% SL at 0.3 ml per litre or Thiamethoxam 25% WG at 0.3 g per litre sprayed at the leaf axils where aphids hide.
diseases.treatment_organic: Plant only certified virus-indexed tissue-culture material. Rogue and destroy infected mats by injecting glyphosate 2 ml + 8 ml water into the pseudostem to kill the whole mat including suckers. Spray neem oil for aphids.
diseases.dosage: Imidacloprid: 60 ml per acre in 200 litres water, focused on the crown and leaf bases. Repeat every 30 days during aphid flights.
diseases.safety_precautions: Glyphosate injection must be done with a marked syringe kept only for that purpose. Do not eat fruit from injected plants. Imidacloprid is toxic to bees — avoid flowering-time sprays.

diseases.name: Onion Purple Blotch
diseases.symptoms: Small white sunken spots appear on leaves and flower stalks; they enlarge into purple-brown zonate lesions with yellow margins. Lesions girdle the leaf and the tip dies back. Severe infection bleaches half the canopy and bulbs stay small.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Warm humid weather of 25-30 C, heavy dew, frequent overhead irrigation, and high nitrogen.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5 g per litre, or Chlorothalonil 75% WP at 2 g per litre, or Hexaconazole 5% EC at 2 ml per litre with sticker. Start at first lesion and repeat at 10-12 day intervals.
diseases.treatment_organic: Spray Trichoderma viride at 5 g per litre. Switch from overhead to drip or furrow irrigation. Add a sticker like soap or 0.5% mineral oil — onion leaves are waxy and shed plain water sprays.
diseases.dosage: Mancozeb + sticker: 1 kg fungicide + 500 ml soap solution per acre in 400 litres water. Three sprays at 10-day intervals during cool wet spells.
diseases.safety_precautions: PPE required. Pre-harvest interval 14 days. Do not spray within 7 days of bulb harvest. Store fungicide away from food and feed.

diseases.name: Onion Thrips
diseases.symptoms: Silvery-white streaks and patches appear on leaves, especially in the inner leaf folds. Leaves curl, twist and dry from the tip. Tiny pale yellow to brown insects (1 mm) are seen running quickly inside the leaf folds when they are pulled apart. Heavy attack stunts bulb growth and invites secondary purple blotch.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Hot dry weather of 30-35 C, low humidity, broken irrigation schedules, and weedy field margins.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Fipronil 5% SC at 2 ml per litre, or Spinosad 45% SC at 0.3 ml per litre, or Lambda-cyhalothrin 5% EC at 1 ml per litre. Add sticker; rotate active ingredients to prevent resistance.
diseases.treatment_organic: Install blue sticky traps at 25 per acre. Spray neem oil 5 ml + soap 1 g per litre weekly. Maintain soil moisture; thrips boom under drought stress. Inter-crop with carrot or coriander to harbour predatory mites.
diseases.dosage: Spinosad: 60 ml per acre in 200 litres water with sticker, sprayed in the early morning when thrips are most active near the leaf tip.
diseases.safety_precautions: Spinosad is safer for natural enemies; rotate with fipronil only when needed. Pre-harvest interval 7 days for spinosad, 21 days for fipronil. PPE required.

diseases.name: Sugarcane Red Rot
diseases.symptoms: Top 3-4 leaves yellow and dry while lower leaves stay green. When a stalk is split lengthwise, the inside pith is brick-red with cross-wise white patches and gives off a sour vinegary or alcohol smell — the diagnostic field sign. Affected canes are hollow, lose juice, and snap easily.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Use of setts from diseased canes, water-logging, heavy rain followed by warm weather, ratoon crops continued beyond 2 years, and susceptible varieties like CO 419, CO 7704.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Treat setts before planting by dipping in Carbendazim 50% WP at 1 g per litre for 10 minutes. Spot drench Carbendazim 1 g per litre at the base of any clump showing top-yellowing, 1 litre per clump.
diseases.treatment_organic: Use resistant varieties like CO 86032 and CO 0238. Hot-water-treat setts at 50 C for 2 hours. Rogue and burn diseased clumps. Avoid ratoons in red-rot-affected fields. Apply Trichoderma viride 5 kg per acre in furrows at planting.
diseases.dosage: Sett dip: 100 g Carbendazim in 100 litres water for one truckload of setts. Field drench: 200 g per acre as needed.
diseases.safety_precautions: Carbendazim is a suspected reproductive toxicant; gloves and mask required. Do not chew or crush sett-dip-treated canes. Pre-harvest interval 60 days for any field use.

diseases.name: Sugarcane Early Shoot Borer
diseases.symptoms: Central whorl of the young shoot dries while the outer leaves stay green — the classic 'dead heart' that pulls out easily and gives off a foul smell. Round bore-holes near the base of the shoot are filled with frass. Affected tillers do not produce a millable cane.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Late planting, dry hot weather of 30-35 C in early stages, light sandy soils, and absence of trash mulching.
diseases.treatment_chemical: Chlorantraniliprole 18.5% SC at 375 ml per hectare as soil application near the base at 30 and 60 DAP. Foliar Carbofuran 3% G at 33 kg per hectare placed in whorls during tillering (where registered).
diseases.treatment_organic: Trash-mulch the rows with sugarcane leaves to suppress moth oviposition. Release Trichogramma chilonis at 50,000 per acre weekly during 30-90 DAP. Light traps with 1 per acre in the first 60 days. Resistant varieties (CO 86032).
diseases.dosage: Chlorantraniliprole: 150 ml per acre as a soil-directed spray in 200 litres water, base-of-clump targeting. One application at 30 DAP and a second only if dead-heart count exceeds 10%.
diseases.safety_precautions: Carbofuran granules are highly toxic — keep away from children, livestock, and water bodies. PPE essential. Pre-harvest interval 90 days for granular applications. Wash hands and clothes immediately after handling.
